sections.title: Welcome
text: Welcome! It looks like you're interested in joining the Gordano Model Flying Club, and we're glad you're here.
As part of our joining process, we ask that all new members come along and meet us first, it's a great opportunity for us to get to know each other.
Please contact us and we'll get a time sorted for you to come down to the club, we'd love to show you around!

Below is intended to be of use if you are looking to learn to fly.  If you're here purely for the RC car action, welcome! your earning curve is a gentler slope (but don't worry, you can make up for it in tyre smoke and sideways grins). Either way, we're all having a ridiculous amount of fun.

One small bit of paperwork before you unleash your inner Stig: car drivers need to be a member of either the British Radio Car Association (BRCA) or the British Model Flying Association (BMFA) to cover the insurance.

sections.title: Training ✈️
text: Nothing would please us more if we could employ a full-time trainer, sadly that’s not really an option.

The training that takes place at the club happens because people have formed relationships with people that are willing to assist.  The best way to form these relationships is to come down to the club and chat with the flyers.
There is nothing to worry about, pack a flask and some sandwiches and pop down. The information you will learn will be priceless and honestly will save you money.
The RC hobby can be a minefield to start with, but you soon pick it up.

A simulator is well worth the investment. There are different simulators on the market and even some free ones. Realflight will appear at the top of many people’s simulator list, and can be bought with a controller. Come down to the club and chat with the flyers, get a feel for what is being used.

During your learning phase, you'll need to be accompanied by a club member who holds a BMFA 'A' Certificate whenever you fly. Once you've earned your own certificate, you're free to fly independently, and with our very own examiner right here at the club, it's a goal that's well within reach.

sections.title: Gear
text: The hobby doesn't have to be expensive, fun is the goal, not spending. That said, you will need a few basics to get started.
Transmitter

This is how you talk to your aircraft, whether that's a plane, helicopter, or drone. For most people, a 6-channel digital transmitter does the job perfectly. You're controlling speed, height, and direction; it really is that simple. You might want more channels down the line, but when you're learning, this is all you need.
Futaba and Spektrum are well known makes that are used at the club.
You can often pick up a good second-hand transmitter on eBay without spending a lot. Not sure where to start? Come along to the club and have a chat with our flyers.
Receiver
The receiver picks up the signal from your transmitter and translates it into action, telling the servos, ESC, and engine exactly what to do.

Transmitters and receivers communicate via a shared protocol, essentially a common language. Just as a French speaker and an English speaker can't easily understand each other, a transmitter and receiver on different protocols won't be able to communicate. Stick to the same protocol on both, and you're good to go.
First Plane

For your first plane, you'll need one that weighs over 1kg, as this is a requirement for getting your BMFA 'A' Certificate . It should also have an undercarriage fitted, so it can take off from the ground unassisted.

A high-wing foam plane is an ideal choice for beginners. It will likely pick up the odd bump along the way, but that's all part of learning, and thankfully, foam is one of the easiest materials to fix.
You can pick one up a second-hand trainer, it’s well worth enquiring at the club as someone might have one to sell.
Charger and Batteries

Every plane needs power, and today that means Lithium polymer (LiPo) batteries. They're compact, powerful, and the go-to choice for the hobby. Your trainer will probably take a 3S 2200mAh battery but pay attention to connectors There are several types out there, and your battery and ESC need to match. The XT-60 is by far the most common, so it's a good one to look out for.

To charge your batteries you'll need a charger, nothing fancy, but it must be capable of safely charging LiPo batteries. Chargers come in two types: AC and DC. A DC-only charger will need a converter if you want to charge from the mains, so opting for one that handles both AC and DC gives you more flexibility, including the handy option of charging from a car battery out at the field.
How many batteries do you need? well that depends on how many flights you want ✈️

sections.title: Prerequisites
text: To be a flying member of Gordano Model Flying Club we require you to be a member of the British Model Flying Association (BFMA) and register with the CAA.

When you register with the CAA you will need to take your Flyer ID test, if you want to fly your own plane you will need to get an Operator ID. You are required to display your Operator ID on any craft you fly. Nothing to worry about, it’s an online test that with a little studying you will be fine.
You are required to display your Operator ID on any craft you fly.

sections.title: Safety
text: Getting into RC as a newcomer or returning after a break can feel like stepping into a whole new world. Today's craft perform at a level that simply wasn't possible before, driven by remarkable advances in battery and motor technology
We use Lithium polymer (LiPo) batteries for our electric models. These are not like the AAA’s you have a home.
Also please remove the propellers from any craft when you are working on it! Fun fact about our club: the average hand has fewer than five fingers. 😂

sections.title: Joining
text: When you are ready to join click the members button below and select the Join Us / Renew button
Once you have filled in all the information, your information will be sent to our membership coordinator for approval
